ir_specs.statement: The apt command provides a high-level commandline interface for the package management system and is intended as an end user interface.
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: apt is described as a high-level end user interface for package management.
ir_specs.notes: APT behavior is distribution-specific to Debian-family systems and may vary by release.

ir_specs.statement: The apt upgrade command is used to install available upgrades of packages currently installed on the system.
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: apt upgrade installs available upgrades for installed packages.
ir_specs.notes: APT behavior is distribution-specific to Debian-family systems and may vary by release.

ir_specs.statement: The apt upgrade command never removes existing installed packages.
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: apt upgrade may add dependencies but will not remove installed packages.
ir_specs.notes: APT behavior is distribution-specific to Debian-family systems and may vary by release.

ir_specs.statement: The apt full-upgrade command may remove currently installed packages if that is needed to upgrade the system as a whole.
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: apt full-upgrade can remove installed packages when required for the upgrade.
ir_specs.notes: APT behavior is distribution-specific to Debian-family systems and may vary by release.

ir_specs.statement: Removing a package with apt leaves usually small modified user configuration files behind.
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: apt remove leaves modified user configuration files behind.
ir_specs.notes: APT behavior is distribution-specific to Debian-family systems and may vary by release.

ir_specs.statement: The apt autoremove command removes packages that were automatically installed to satisfy dependencies and are no longer needed.
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: apt autoremove removes no-longer-needed automatically installed dependency packages.
ir_specs.notes: APT behavior is distribution-specific to Debian-family systems and may vary by release.

ir_specs.statement: The apt search command can be used to search for given regex terms in the list of available packages and display matches.
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: apt search looks for regex terms in available packages and shows matches.
ir_specs.notes: APT behavior is distribution-specific to Debian-family systems and may vary by release.

ir_specs.statement: The apt list command can display installed, upgradeable, or all available versions of packages.
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: apt list supports installed, upgradeable, and all-versions views.
ir_specs.notes: APT behavior is distribution-specific to Debian-family systems and may vary by release.

ir_specs.statement: The apt edit-sources command lets you edit sources.list files in your preferred text editor while providing basic sanity checks.
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: apt edit-sources edits sources.list files with basic sanity checks.
ir_specs.notes: APT behavior is distribution-specific to Debian-family systems and may vary by release.

ir_specs.statement: The apt-get update command resynchronizes the package index files from their sources.
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: apt-get update refreshes package index files from configured sources.
ir_specs.notes: APT behavior is distribution-specific to Debian-family systems and may vary by release.

ir_specs.statement: An apt-get update should always be performed before apt-get upgrade or apt-get dist-upgrade.
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: The apt-get man page says update should always come before upgrade or dist-upgrade.
ir_specs.notes: APT behavior is distribution-specific to Debian-family systems and may vary by release.

ir_specs.statement: The apt-get install command retrieves and installs packages required by the specified package or packages.
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: apt-get install also brings in required packages for the requested package set.
ir_specs.notes: APT behavior is distribution-specific to Debian-family systems and may vary by release.

ir_specs.statement: The apt-get remove command removes packages but leaves configuration files on the system.
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: apt-get remove leaves configuration files behind.
ir_specs.notes: APT behavior is distribution-specific to Debian-family systems and may vary by release.

ir_specs.statement: The apt-get purge command removes packages and deletes configuration files too.
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: apt-get purge removes packages and their configuration files.
ir_specs.notes: APT behavior is distribution-specific to Debian-family systems and may vary by release.

ir_specs.statement: The apt-get download command downloads the given binary package into the current directory.
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: apt-get download fetches a binary package into the current directory.
ir_specs.notes: APT behavior is distribution-specific to Debian-family systems and may vary by release.

ir_specs.statement: The apt-get clean command removes everything but the lock file from /var/cache/apt/archives/ and /var/cache/apt/archives/partial/.
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: apt-get clean clears retrieved package files but keeps the lock file.
ir_specs.notes: APT behavior is distribution-specific to Debian-family systems and may vary by release.

ir_specs.statement: The apt-get autoclean command removes only retrieved package files that can no longer be downloaded.
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: apt-get autoclean targets obsolete cached package files instead of clearing everything.
ir_specs.notes: APT behavior is distribution-specific to Debian-family systems and may vary by release.

ir_specs.statement: The apt-get check command is a diagnostic tool that updates the package cache and checks for broken dependencies.
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: apt-get check updates cache metadata and checks dependency consistency.
ir_specs.notes: APT behavior is distribution-specific to Debian-family systems and may vary by release.

ir_specs.statement: The apt-get --download-only option retrieves package files but does not unpack or install them.
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: apt-get --download-only fetches packages without unpacking or installing them.
ir_specs.notes: APT behavior is distribution-specific to Debian-family systems and may vary by release.

ir_specs.statement: The apt-get --only-upgrade option does not install new packages and only upgrades packages that are already installed.
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: apt-get --only-upgrade limits install to packages that already exist on the system.
ir_specs.notes: APT behavior is distribution-specific to Debian-family systems and may vary by release.

ir_specs.statement: The apt-get --no-upgrade option prevents command-line packages from being upgraded if they are already installed.
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: apt-get --no-upgrade blocks upgrades of already installed command-line packages.
ir_specs.notes: APT behavior is distribution-specific to Debian-family systems and may vary by release.

ir_specs.statement: When used with upgrade, the apt-get --with-new-pkgs option allows installing new dependencies but never removes packages.
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: apt-get --with-new-pkgs can add new dependency packages during upgrade without allowing removals.
ir_specs.notes: APT behavior is distribution-specific to Debian-family systems and may vary by release.

ir_specs.statement: DNF is a package manager for RPM-based Linux distributions.
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: DNF is documented as a package manager for RPM-based Linux distributions.
ir_specs.notes: DNF behavior is distribution-specific to RPM-based systems and plugin behavior can vary by environment.

ir_specs.statement: DNF returns 0 when the operation was successful.
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: DNF uses exit code 0 for success.
ir_specs.notes: DNF behavior is distribution-specific to RPM-based systems and plugin behavior can vary by environment.

ir_specs.statement: DNF uses exit code 100 for the check-update case.
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: DNF reserves exit code 100 for check-update.
ir_specs.notes: DNF behavior is distribution-specific to RPM-based systems and plugin behavior can vary by environment.

ir_specs.statement: The dnf --allowerasing option allows erasing installed packages to resolve dependencies.
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: dnf --allowerasing permits package removals during dependency resolution.
ir_specs.notes: DNF behavior is distribution-specific to RPM-based systems and plugin behavior can vary by environment.

ir_specs.statement: The dnf --assumeno option automatically answers no for all questions.
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: dnf --assumeno declines prompts automatically.
ir_specs.notes: DNF behavior is distribution-specific to RPM-based systems and plugin behavior can vary by environment.

ir_specs.statement: The dnf --best option forces DNF to consider only the latest packages for directly requested packages and fail if the latest version cannot be installed.
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: dnf --best prefers latest versions for requested packages and fails when the latest cannot be installed.
ir_specs.notes: DNF behavior is distribution-specific to RPM-based systems and plugin behavior can vary by environment.

ir_specs.statement: The dnf --cacheonly option runs entirely from the system cache and uses it even when it is expired.
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: dnf --cacheonly avoids cache refreshes and works from existing cache data.
ir_specs.notes: DNF behavior is distribution-specific to RPM-based systems and plugin behavior can vary by environment.

ir_specs.statement: The dnf --disablerepo option temporarily disables active repositories for the current dnf command.
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: dnf --disablerepo only affects the current command invocation.
ir_specs.notes: DNF behavior is distribution-specific to RPM-based systems and plugin behavior can vary by environment.

question_specs.question: The `apt` command is documented as a high-level command-line interface intended for end users.
question_specs.explanation: The `apt` man page describes it as a high-level end user interface.

question_specs.question: The `apt upgrade` command only refreshes package metadata and does not install package upgrades.
question_specs.explanation: `apt upgrade` is used to install available upgrades of installed packages.

question_specs.question: The `apt upgrade` command does not remove existing installed packages.
question_specs.explanation: The manual explicitly says existing packages will never be removed by `apt upgrade`.

question_specs.question: The `apt full-upgrade` command never removes installed packages.
question_specs.explanation: `apt full-upgrade` may remove installed packages when needed to upgrade the system as a whole.

question_specs.question: Removing a package with `apt remove` can leave modified user configuration files behind.
question_specs.explanation: The `apt` man page says removing a package usually leaves small modified user configuration files behind.

question_specs.question: The `apt autoremove` command removes only packages that the user explicitly installed by hand.
question_specs.explanation: `apt autoremove` targets packages that were automatically installed as dependencies and are no longer needed.

question_specs.question: The `apt search` command can search available packages for regex terms and display matches.
question_specs.explanation: The `apt` documentation says `search` can search for given regex terms and display matches.

question_specs.question: The `apt list` command can show only installed packages, not upgradeable or all available versions.
question_specs.explanation: `apt list` supports installed, upgradeable, and all-versions views.

question_specs.question: The `apt edit-sources` command edits `sources.list` files and provides basic sanity checks.
question_specs.explanation: That is the documented purpose of `apt edit-sources`.

question_specs.question: The `apt-get update` command installs every upgradable package on the system.
question_specs.explanation: `apt-get update` resynchronizes package index files; it does not itself install upgrades.

question_specs.question: The `apt-get` documentation says `update` should be performed before `upgrade` or `dist-upgrade`.
question_specs.explanation: The man page explicitly recommends running `update` before `upgrade` or `dist-upgrade`.

question_specs.question: The `apt-get install` command never retrieves dependency packages automatically.
question_specs.explanation: `apt-get install` retrieves and installs the packages required by the requested package set.

question_specs.question: The `apt-get remove` command removes a package but leaves configuration files on the system.
question_specs.explanation: That is the documented difference between `remove` and `purge`.

question_specs.question: The `apt-get purge` command keeps configuration files in place so the package can be restored quickly.
question_specs.explanation: `apt-get purge` removes packages and deletes configuration files too.

question_specs.question: The `apt-get download` command downloads the requested binary package into the current directory.
question_specs.explanation: The man page says `download` puts the binary package in the current directory.

question_specs.question: The `apt-get clean` command leaves all retrieved package files in the cache.
question_specs.explanation: `apt-get clean` removes everything but the lock file from the package cache directories.

question_specs.question: The `apt-get autoclean` command removes only package files that can no longer be downloaded.
question_specs.explanation: Unlike `clean`, `autoclean` removes only obsolete cached package files.

question_specs.question: The `apt-get check` command automatically reinstalls packages to fix dependency problems.
question_specs.explanation: `apt-get check` is a diagnostic tool that updates the cache and checks for broken dependencies.

question_specs.question: The `apt-get --download-only` option retrieves package files without unpacking or installing them.
question_specs.explanation: That option downloads packages only and does not unpack or install them.

question_specs.question: With `apt-get --only-upgrade`, `install` can install a brand new package that is not already present.
question_specs.explanation: `--only-upgrade` avoids installing new packages and upgrades only already installed ones.

question_specs.question: The `apt-get --no-upgrade` option prevents already installed command-line packages from being upgraded.
question_specs.explanation: That is the documented effect of `--no-upgrade` when used with `install`.

question_specs.question: When used with `upgrade`, `apt-get --with-new-pkgs` allows package removals if dependency resolution needs them.
question_specs.explanation: The option may allow new packages to be installed, but it does not allow removals.

question_specs.question: DNF is documented as a package manager for RPM-based Linux distributions.
question_specs.explanation: The DNF command reference describes DNF that way.

question_specs.question: DNF exit code `0` means an error occurred but DNF handled it.
question_specs.explanation: In DNF, exit code `0` means the operation was successful.

question_specs.question: DNF uses exit code `100` for the `check-update` case.
question_specs.explanation: The command reference lists `100` with the note to see `check-update`.

question_specs.question: The DNF `--allowerasing` option forbids erasing installed packages during dependency resolution.
question_specs.explanation: `--allowerasing` does the opposite: it allows erasing installed packages to resolve dependencies.

question_specs.question: The DNF `--assumeno` option automatically answers no to all questions.
question_specs.explanation: That is the explicit behavior of `--assumeno`.

question_specs.question: The DNF `--best` option lets DNF silently settle for an older directly requested package when the latest version cannot be installed.
question_specs.explanation: For directly requested packages, `--best` forces the latest choice and fails if it cannot be installed.

question_specs.question: The DNF `--cacheonly` option runs from cache without updating it, even if the cache is expired.
question_specs.explanation: The reference says `--cacheonly` uses the system cache entirely, even when expired.

question_specs.question: The DNF `--disablerepo` option permanently disables repositories until they are manually re-enabled later.
question_specs.explanation: The option only temporarily disables active repositories for the current DNF command.
